title: How America Broke Its Own Military
excerpt: The most powerful army on earth was stretched past its limits — not by its enemies, but by the politicians who drove it into unwinnable wars.
content_html:
On 10 September 2001, Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld stood in front of the assembled great and good of the Pentagon and delivered an expansive lecture entitled ‘Bureaucracy to Battlefield’. Its prescriptions were radical — among the most portentous in US military history — but thanks to the terrorist atrocities the following day, his words remain buried deep in the memory hole, while their consequences are buried under the sands of Iraq and Afghanistan. ‘The topic today is an adversary that poses a threat, a serious threat, to the security of the United States of America’, Rumsfeld began, before revealing the threat to be not Al-Qaeda, but the ‘Pentagon bureaucracy.’ ‘Not the people, but the processes’, he added reassuringly. ‘Not the civilians, but the systems. Not the men and women in uniform, but the uniformity of thought and action that we too often impose on them.’ In essence, Rumsfeld’s speech that day was designed to lay the ground and soften up his workers for a massive privatization of the Department of Defense’s services. It was the realization of a long-held dream for Republican politicians and their corporate allies in Washington — who would now be presented with a sweet shop full of lucrative government contacts to chew on.

With the tragic events of 9/11 and the ensuing two-front ground war in the Middle East, Rumsfeld got an opportunity to enact his programme with minimal opposition. The results were clear eight years into the War on Terror, when the DOD had 95,461 private contractors working for them in Iraq compared to 95,900 US military personnel. The use of private contractors was by then so embedded that Barack Obama’s pledge on the campaign trail in 2008 to bring an end to the practice became just another broken promise among many. But while the privatisation of the war effort is a topic that has been explored extensively by a number of journalists, notably Jeremy Scahill, one aspect of the program has received little coverage — namely Rumsfeld’s plan for soldiers on the payroll of the DOD. This was a scheme that would prove catastrophic for the troops and the occupied populations living under them. Veiled in the language of business-style efficiency savings, Rumsfeld’s plan was intended to eviscerate the US military, which was to become merely an appendage to the massive private forces the US would soon employ.

‘In this period of limited funds,’ Rumsfeld continued, ‘we need every nickel, every good idea, every innovation, every effort to help modernize and transform the US military.’ This could only be done by changing the basics of how the Pentagon worked, in a process that would later be dubbed ‘Transformation’: ‘Many of the skills we most require are also in high demand in the private sector, as all of you know. To compete, we need to bring the Department of Defense the human resources practices that have already transformed the private sector.’ Even the DOD itself was to be run like a corporation: ‘We must employ the tools of modern business. More flexible compensation packages, modern recruiting techniques and better training.’ What Rumsfeld wanted was a scaled-down, streamlined US military — a reversal of what had become known as the Powell Doctrine, named for the Desert Storm general Colin Powell, who believed in high troop numbers, ‘overwhelming force’, and a defined exit strategy.

It was a risky approach for Rumsfeld to take. Even before 9/11, Powell, by now Secretary of State, had observed that ‘Our armed forces are stretched rather thin, and there is a limit to how many of these deployments we can sustain.’ That would prove to be an understatement. But Rumsfeld was backed in his new approach by his boss, President George W. Bush, who much to Powell’s consternation shared the same vision: ‘Building tomorrow’s force is not going to be easy. Changing the direction of our military is like changing the course of a mighty ship’, Bush said in May 2001.

The oft-repeated cliché is that everything changed on September 11, and indeed it did for millions of Americans. But not for Rumsfeld: his priorities stayed the same, while his popularity surged as he was pictured helping victims of the attack at the Pentagon into ambulances. He now had not only the ultimate cover for changing the course of the mighty ship and designing a pared-down business-style war machine, but also the perfect laboratory for his experiments. It wasn’t long before the war drums began beating in earnest soon. The invasion of Afghanistan began with a Rumsfeld-inspired Special Forces mission to bribe local warlords, supported by airstrikes obviating the need for overwhelming manpower. But the so-called neoconservatives weren’t finished yet: they had their eyes on the ultimate prize of Iraq and, sure enough, eighteen months later and much more controversially, the country with the third-largest reserves of oil in the world was attacked — a move Rumsfeld’s deputy Paul Wolfowitz had been advocating for (again, with a Rumsfeld-style small force) in the 1990s. Over the next decade, US bombs fell on Syria and Pakistan and Yemen, amongst others, as the Middle East turned into a conflagration of Dantesque proportions, with civilians, insurgents, and US service members all caught up in the blaze.

Rumsfeld’s guinea-pigs for his new experiment in ‘flexible’ military planning were the patriotic Americans who believed they were signing up to defend their country — at the height of the War on Terror, 1.6 million of them had served in the Middle East. That number, equivalent to the population of a country like Estonia or a city like Philadelphia, must have come as a surprise to Rumsfeld, who had predicated his whole war plan on a much smaller force and a short war. In fact, soon after 9/11, General Tommy Franks had been asked by Rumsfeld to estimate how many troops an invasion of Iraq would require. The last contingency plan for invading Iraq, dating from 1998, had recommended a force of more than 380,000. But, apparently under pressure from Rumsfeld, General Franks presented his ‘Generated Start’ plan with an initial troop count of just 275,000. Even that was too much for Rumsfeld, who in typical peremptory fashion slapped down the general and pushed for an even smaller force.

According to Michael Gordon, military correspondent for the New York Times, ‘They came up with a variant called the running start, where you begin maybe with a division or so, and then the reinforcements would flow behind it. So, you start small but you just keep sending more of what you need.’ This approach generated what was called a ‘war within a war’, as it pitted Rumsfeld against General Eric Shinseki, the army’s popular Chief of Staff, and Secretary of State Powell, both of whom (rightly) believed that large numbers of troops would be needed to ensure security after the initial fighting was over. General Shinseki publicly proposed a force of 200,000, after his frustration with Rumsfeld’s intransigence on the issue became insufferable. It didn’t matter. Rumsfeld wouldn’t listen.

This tactic was so blinded by ideology that it directly contradicted the ostensible goals of the invasion — disarming Saddam Hussein. ‘The United States did not have nearly enough troops to secure the hundreds of suspected WMD sites that had supposedly been identified in Iraq or to secure the nation’s long, porous borders’, wrote Michael Gordon and General Bernard Trainor in their book Cobra II. ‘Had the Iraqis possessed WMD and terrorist groups been prevalent in Iraq as the Bush administration so loudly asserted, [the limited number of] U.S. forces might well have failed to prevent the WMD from being spirited out of the country and falling into the hands of the dark forces the administration had declared war against.’ But it wasn’t just ideology that was to blame for the ensuing damage to the US military and its war aims; there was a sizeable dose of incompetence as well. Declassified war-planning documents from the US Central Command in August 2002 show how ill-prepared the Bush administration was for the occupation which was to follow. The plan they put together assumed that by December 2006 the US military would be almost completely drawn down from Iraq, leaving a residual force of just 5,000 troops. It was madness, but nevertheless music to Rumsfeld’s ears.

After much wrangling, Rumsfeld and Franks compromised and the initial force numbered 130,000, much smaller than had been envisioned in the 1990s, but not as slim as Rumsfeld had hoped. It didn’t go to plan anyway, and the troop levels were not enough to get a handle on this country of 30 million people. Incrementally more service members were sent out alongside private contractors as the situation descended into chaos after the first viceroy Paul Bremner’s decommissioning of the police and military sparked endless violence. By 2005, the US had 150,000 troops deployed in Iraq, and 19,500 in Afghanistan. But the war plan meant the military wasn’t prepared in any way for this kind of deployment — and it was unraveling. In 2005, just two years into the war in Iraq, people were talking openly about the fact that the US military had reached breaking point. At a Senate hearing in March of that year, General Richard A. Cody expressed these concerns publicly: ‘What keeps me awake at night is what will this all-volunteer force look like in 2007.’ But he didn’t know the half of it. Worse was to come as in the same year the army missed its recruitment targets by the largest margin since 1979, a time when US society was still afflicted with so-called ‘Vietnam Syndrome’ and the army was much bigger and recruiting twice as many soldiers. 

Around this time a retired army officer named Andrew F. Krepinevich, writing under a Pentagon contract, released a shocking report which was scathing about the US military being able to maintain its troop levels in Iraq without breaking the military or losing the war. His diagnosis was simple: the US armed forces were ‘confronted with a protracted deployment against irregular forces waging insurgencies’, but the ground forces required to provide stability and security in Afghanistan and Iraq ‘clearly exceed those available for the mission.’ To compensate, the army was introducing change by the back door at the expense of their troops. Krepinevich pointed to the frequent and untimely redeployment of service members. ‘Soldiers and brigades are being deployed more frequently, and for longer periods, than what the Army believes is appropriate in order to attract and retain the number of soldiers necessary to maintain the size and quality of the force asking.’ He offered three solutions for overcoming the desperate shortage of troops: redeploy existing troops more frequently still; redeploy them for even longer; or deploy US Marine ground troops.

The first two of these prescriptions had already been undertaken by the army, but it was a dangerous game. ‘How often can a soldier be put in harm’s way and still desire to remain in the Army?’ Krepinevich asked. ‘It is not clear, even to Army leaders, how long this practice can be sustained without inducing recruitment and retention problems.’ His conclusions were not optimistic: the army, he said, was ‘in a race against time’ in which ‘its ability to execute long-term initiatives’ was compromised by the risk of ‘breaking the force in the form of a catastrophic decline in recruitment and retention.’

He continued that it would be difficult not to stress the active and reserve components ‘so severely that recruiting and retention problems become so severe as to threaten the effectiveness of the force.’ By now, then, everyone — including even Rumsfeld himself — knew the army had to increase in size. ‘With today’s demands placing such a high strain on our service members, it becomes more crucial than ever that we work to alleviate their burden’, said Representative Ike Stelton (D-Mis), who had a long track record of advocating for a bigger army. But with recruitment down and anger at the war widespread how could this be done?

There was one course of action that would have instantly sewn up the military’s unraveling seams, namely: the draft. But it was too controversial. Involuntary conscription had been abolished by Congress in 1973 at the end of the Vietnam War. At the time Krepinevich was writing, it did enter into the national conversation, although the Bush administration remained implacably opposed for reasons of naked self-interest. When Rumsfeld testified at a Senate Appropriations Defense Subcommittee in April 2005 the issue was raised by Democratic Senator Daniel Inouye. ‘For the first time in many years the Army and Marine Corps are not meeting their recruiting targets. There are some who are already discussing the draft’, he said with diplomatic tact. An exasperated Rumsfeld shuffled forward in his seat and put his mouth closer to the microphone: ‘I think the only people who could conceivably be talking about a draft are people who are speaking from pinnacles of near-perfect ignorance’, he replied. ‘The last thing we need is a draft. We just don’t.’

The Democrats kept pushing until Charles Rangel, a Congressman from New York, reintroduced the Draft Bill in February 2006, which if passed would have reinstated conscription for all those up to 42-years-old. ‘Every day that the military option is on the table, as declared by the president in his State of the Union address, in Iran, North Korea, and Syria, reinstatement of the military draft is an option that must also be considered, whether we like it or not’, said Rangel. ‘If the military is already having trouble getting the recruits they need, what can we do to fill the ranks if the war spreads from Iraq to other countries? We may have no other choice but a draft.’ It was rejected by congress, much to the delight of President Bush. ‘I applaud the House of Representatives for soundly rejecting the ‘Reinstate the Draft’ bill’, he said in the aftermath. ‘If this bill were presented to me, I would veto it. America’s all-volunteer military is the best in the world, and reinstating the draft would be bad policy. We have increased pay and benefits to ensure that our troops have the resources they need to fight and win the war on terror. I want every American to understand that, as long as I am President, there will be no draft.’

Reinstating the draft wouldn’t have made the US an anomaly amongst its allies: many still run compulsory conscription programs, including Israel and (until 2011) Germany. Yet in a war so unpopular and with a president under so much pressure, the administration was aware that conscripting the nation’s youth could well be the straw that would break the camel’s back. Denied the draft, the military fell back on methods from earlier attempts to swell the ranks during Vietnam: not only recruiting among society’s poorest, but also changing the regulations on enlistment. In 1965, as the troop build up in South Vietnam grew, the military started to abandon its standards for recruitment and hundreds of thousands of men who scored among the lowest IQ percentiles were admitted for the first time. ‘Prior to American escalation in Vietnam such men were routinely rejected, but with a war on these “new standards” were suddenly declared fit to fight. Rejection rates plummeted’, writes one historian. In 1966, then-Secretary of Defense Robert McNamara instituted a program called ‘Project 100,000’ which aimed to increase the levels of troops by that number within two years by admitting civilians who had previously not met the required standards.

Without conscription and with recruitment targets being consistently missed, the Bush administration and the Pentagon devised a similar plan. By subcontracting out myriad operational tasks to private military institutions like Blackwater and DynCorp, who received billions of dollars in government largesse to man the frontlines in the War on Terror, the government had partially dealt with the gap between its demand for cannon fodder and the supply of quality troops. A thin coalition of countries, later including NATO troops in Afghanistan and the UN in Iraq, helped ameliorate the chronic troop deficits. But it wasn’t enough. In 2004, Bush took another unusual step to plug the hole: he called up 37,000 members of the National Guard to go to fight in the deserts of Iraq. Not since the Korean War had there been such a mobilization: the National Guard had served in the Gulf War and Kosovo, but in nowhere near such huge numbers. By June 2005, they accounted for 45 percent of the total army in Iraq. Then there were the monetary inducements, which became quite lucrative for service members and crippling for the Pentagon. The Rand Corporation conducted an in-depth study which found that the DOD budget for enlistment and reenlistment bonuses had skyrocketed between 2000 to 2008, more than doubling to $1.4 billion for reenlistment bonuses. The average army enlistment bonus increased from $5,600 to about $18,000 per soldier over the same period. In just three years, from 2006 to 2009, the army dispensed with $1 billion to recruit 64,526 active-duty and reserve soldiers. Army Reserve recruits saw their bonuses more than double over the same period, to $19,500. But the study also found that in the army, recruiters themselves tend to be a more cost-effective way to swell the ranks of the military than enlistment bonuses or pay increases. The Bush administration took that route too. To achieve its 2007 goals, the army increased its 8,000-strong recruiting force with 2,000 new assistants.

At the same time — and perhaps most importantly — those who should have been kicked out were being allowed to stay. In June 2005, the Wall Street Journal turned up an internal memo to senior commanders which called the growing dropout rate — called ‘attrition’ in military jargon — ‘a matter of great concern’. ‘We need your concerted effort to reverse the negative trend’, it read. ‘By reducing attrition 1 percent, we can save up to 3,000 initial-term soldiers. That’s 3,000 more soldiers in our formations.’ It was an explicit call to drop standards: the message being that soldiers addicted to alcohol and drugs, those who lose their fitness, or their mental poise, shouldn’t be discharged. It was batten down the hatches time. The WSJ quoted a battalion commander as saying: ‘It is the guys on weight control . . . school no-shows, drug users, etc., who eat up my time and cause my hair to grey prematurely . . . Often they have more than one of these issues simultaneously.’

Such sentiments did not exist in a vacuum. Rumsfeld and the Pentagon were, in fact, allowing the dismantling of the whole regulatory structure for enlistment and retention that the US armed forces had built up in the 25 years since Vietnam. The slim military needed fattening up and this was the only way to do it. In the end it constituted a complete re-evaluation of who was qualified to serve in the US armed forces, a full-works facelift of the service unheard of in the annals of modern American history. In the relatively halcyon days of the First Gulf War in 1990, the US military blocked the enlistment of felons. It spurned men and women with low IQ’s or those without a high-school diploma. It would either block the enlistment of or kick out neo-Nazis and gang-members. It would treat or discharge alcoholics, drug abusers, and the mentally ill. It would pass up the services of foreign citizens to fight its wars. No more. While the Bush administration adopted conservative policies pretty much universally, it saved its ration of liberalism for the US military, where it scrapped all the previously sacrosanct regulations governing recruitment to the most powerful fighting force in the world. Under the aegis of the War on Terror, the US armed forces became a Mecca for the ‘different’, the weird and wonderful (and dangerous) of America.

Throughout all this, however, the military maintained a rictus, everything-in-order, smile for the public. As late as August 2007, when the crisis befalling the institution had become widely known, Michael Dominguez, Principal Deputy Under Secretary of Defense for Personnel and Readiness, began his testimony to a Congressional hearing with a slap on the back for the US military: ‘Let me begin by acknowledging an historic achievement that many, including some of our own experts, would have thought impossible a few years ago’, he said. ‘We have taken an all-volunteer military to war. We have done it in a strong economy with 4.5 percent unemployment. We often have asked that force and their families to do more on short notice. And through it all, we have manned this nation’s military with people far above average relative to their peers.” It was simply untrue. The average member of the US military was no longer far above their average peer. The Bush administration, together with a pliant Pentagon (and the succeeding Obama administration), actually enabled the US military to undergo the biggest and fastest transformation in its history.

And it was not just American soldiers who were short-changed by Rumsfeld’s vision — the occupied populations were sacrificed with rivers of their own blood. Many of the wars’ worst atrocities are linked directly to the loosening of enlistment regulations on criminals, racist extremists, and gang members, amongst others. Then there is the domestic US population, which has had to put up with military-trained gang members marauding around their cities; as well as Mexican civilians who have paid with their lives in the drug wars facilitated in part by the US military. The effects of this will be felt for decades to come. Finally, there’s the safety of the troops themselves. Loosening standards on intelligence and body weight, for example, compromised the military’s operational readiness and undoubtedly endangered the lives of American and allied troops. Thousands of young Americans have paid with their lives for this folly.

US society changed profoundly after the September 11 attacks and during the subsequent wars. In many ways, the military is a reflection of the society from which it is drawn, and the changes in the composition of the US military and its regulations over this period reflected a country in political, cultural, and economic reverse gear. As America became increasingly bigoted and inward-looking, so radicalism in the US military increased. As young people became ever fatter, so too did the soldiers. As the criminal justice system locked more and more people up, so the military had to increase the numbers of felons it allowed in. ‘Today’s young men and women are more overweight . . . and are being charged for offenses that in earlier years wouldn’t have been considered a serious offense, and might not have resulted in charges in the first place’, an army spokesman complained in 2008. At the height of the War on Terror, only one in three men in the general population met the pre-9/11 physical, mental, educational, and other eligibility requirements needed to enlist in the armed forces. ‘The numbers of people who meet our enlistment standards is astonishingly low’, grumbled Under Secretary Dominguez at the end of his testimony lauding the military’s success. To enlist the rest, the US military had to change in profound and dangerous ways.

This is an edited extract from Matt Kennard’s book Irregular Army: How the US Military Recruited Neo-Nazis, Gang Members, and Criminals, recently published by OR Books in a new and expanded edition.
content_text: ‘I just can’t imagine someone looking at the United States armed forces today and suggesting that they are close to breaking.’ — Donald Rumsfeld, 2006On 10 September 2001, Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld stood in front of the assembled great and good of the Pentagon and delivered an expansive lecture entitled ‘Bureaucracy to Battlefield’. Its prescriptions were radical — among the most portentous in US military history — but thanks to the terrorist atrocities the following day, his words remain buried deep in the memory hole, while their consequences are buried under the sands of Iraq and Afghanistan. ‘The topic today is an adversary that poses a threat, a serious threat, to the security of the United States of America’, Rumsfeld began, before revealing the threat to be not Al-Qaeda, but the ‘Pentagon bureaucracy.’ ‘Not the people, but the processes’, he added reassuringly. ‘Not the civilians, but the systems. Not the men and women in uniform, but the uniformity of thought and action that we too often impose on them.’ In essence, Rumsfeld’s speech that day was designed to lay the ground and soften up his workers for a massive privatization of the Department of Defense’s services. It was the realization of a long-held dream for Republican politicians and their corporate allies in Washington — who would now be presented with a sweet shop full of lucrative government contacts to chew on.With the tragic events of 9/11 and the ensuing two-front ground war in the Middle East, Rumsfeld got an opportunity to enact his programme with minimal opposition. When Rumsfeld testified at a Senate Appropriations Defense Subcommittee in April 2005 the issue was raised by Democratic Senator Daniel Inouye. ‘For the first time in many years the Army and Marine Corps are not meeting their recruiting targets. There are some who are already discussing the draft’, he said with diplomatic tact. An exasperated Rumsfeld shuffled forward in his seat and put his mouth closer to the microphone: ‘I think the only people who could conceivably be talking about a draft are people who are speaking from pinnacles of near-perfect ignorance’, he replied. ‘The last thing we need is a draft. We just don’t.’The Democrats kept pushing until Charles Rangel, a Congressman from New York, reintroduced the Draft Bill in February 2006, which if passed would have reinstated conscription for all those up to 42-years-old. ‘Every day that the military option is on the table, as declared by the president in his State of the Union address, in Iran, North Korea, and Syria, reinstatement of the military draft is an option that must also be considered, whether we like it or not’, said Rangel. ‘If the military is already having trouble getting the recruits they need, what can we do to fill the ranks if the war spreads from Iraq to other countries? We may have no other choice but a draft.’ It was rejected by congress, much to the delight of President Bush. ‘I applaud the House of Representatives for soundly rejecting the ‘Reinstate the Draft’ bill’, he said in the aftermath. ‘If this bill were presented to me, I would veto it. America’s all-volunteer military is the best in the world, and reinstating the draft would be bad policy. We have increased pay and benefits to ensure that our troops have the resources they need to fight and win the war on terror. I want every American to understand that, as long as I am President, there will be no draft.’Reinstating the draft wouldn’t have made the US an anomaly amongst its allies: many still run compulsory conscription programs, including Israel and (until 2011) Germany. Yet in a war so unpopular and with a president under so much pressure, the administration was aware that conscripting the nation’s youth could well be the straw that would break the camel’s back. Denied the draft, the military fell back on methods from earlier attempts to swell the ranks during Vietnam: not only recruiting among society’s poorest, but also changing the regulations on enlistment. In 1965, as the troop build up in South Vietnam grew, the military started to abandon its standards for recruitment and hundreds of thousands of men who scored among the lowest IQ percentiles were admitted for the first time. ‘Prior to American escalation in Vietnam such men were routinely rejected, but with a war on these “new standards” were suddenly declared fit to fight. Rejection rates plummeted’, writes one historian. In 1966, then-Secretary of Defense Robert McNamara instituted a program called ‘Project 100,000’ which aimed to increase the levels of troops by that number within two years by admitting civilians who had previously not met the required standards.Without conscription and with recruitment targets being consistently missed, the Bush administration and the Pentagon devised a similar plan. By subcontracting out myriad operational tasks to private military institutions like Blackwater and DynCorp, who received billions of dollars in government largesse to man the frontlines in the War on Terror, the government had partially dealt with the gap between its demand for cannon fodder and the supply of quality troops. A thin coalition of countries, later including NATO troops in Afghanistan and the UN in Iraq, helped ameliorate the chronic troop deficits. But it wasn’t enough. In 2004, Bush took another unusual step to plug the hole: he called up 37,000 members of the National Guard to go to fight in the deserts of Iraq. Not since the Korean War had there been such a mobilization: the National Guard had served in the Gulf War and Kosovo, but in nowhere near such huge numbers. By June 2005, they accounted for 45 percent of the total army in Iraq. Then there were the monetary inducements, which became quite lucrative for service members and crippling for the Pentagon. The Rand Corporation conducted an in-depth study which found that the DOD budget for enlistment and reenlistment bonuses had skyrocketed between 2000 to 2008, more than doubling to $1.4 billion for reenlistment bonuses. The average army enlistment bonus increased from $5,600 to about $18,000 per soldier over the same period. In just three years, from 2006 to 2009, the army dispensed with $1 billion to recruit 64,526 active-duty and reserve soldiers. Army Reserve recruits saw their bonuses more than double over the same period, to $19,500. But the study also found that in the army, recruiters themselves tend to be a more cost-effective way to swell the ranks of the military than enlistment bonuses or pay increases. The Bush administration took that route too. To achieve its 2007 goals, the army increased its 8,000-strong recruiting force with 2,000 new assistants.At the same time — and perhaps most importantly — those who should have been kicked out were being allowed to stay. In June 2005, the Wall Street Journal turned up an internal memo to senior commanders which called the growing dropout rate — called ‘attrition’ in military jargon — ‘a matter of great concern’. ‘We need your concerted effort to reverse the negative trend’, it read. ‘By reducing attrition 1 percent, we can save up to 3,000 initial-term soldiers. That’s 3,000 more soldiers in our formations.’ It was an explicit call to drop standards: the message being that soldiers addicted to alcohol and drugs, those who lose their fitness, or their mental poise, shouldn’t be discharged. It was batten down the hatches time. The WSJ quoted a battalion commander as saying: ‘It is the guys on weight control . . . school no-shows, drug users, etc., who eat up my time and cause my hair to grey prematurely . . . Often they have more than one of these issues simultaneously.’Such sentiments did not exist in a vacuum. Rumsfeld and the Pentagon were, in fact, allowing the dismantling of the whole regulatory structure for enlistment and retention that the US armed forces had built up in the 25 years since Vietnam. The slim military needed fattening up and this was the only way to do it. In the end it constituted a complete re-evaluation of who was qualified to serve in the US armed forces, a full-works facelift of the service unheard of in the annals of modern American history. In the relatively halcyon days of the First Gulf War in 1990, the US military blocked the enlistment of felons. It spurned men and women with low IQ’s or those without a high-school diploma. It would either block the enlistment of or kick out neo-Nazis and gang-members. It would treat or discharge alcoholics, drug abusers, and the mentally ill. It would pass up the services of foreign citizens to fight its wars. No more. While the Bush administration adopted conservative policies pretty much universally, it saved its ration of liberalism for the US military, where it scrapped all the previously sacrosanct regulations governing recruitment to the most powerful fighting force in the world. Under the aegis of the War on Terror, the US armed forces became a Mecca for the ‘different’, the weird and wonderful (and dangerous) of America.Throughout all this, however, the military maintained a rictus, everything-in-order, smile for the public. As late as August 2007, when the crisis befalling the institution had become widely known, Michael Dominguez, Principal Deputy Under Secretary of Defense for Personnel and Readiness, began his testimony to a Congressional hearing with a slap on the back for the US military: ‘Let me begin by acknowledging an historic achievement that many, including some of our own experts, would have thought impossible a few years ago’, he said. ‘We have taken an all-volunteer military to war. We have done it in a strong economy with 4.5 percent unemployment. We often have asked that force and their families to do more on short notice. And through it all, we have manned this nation’s military with people far above average relative to their peers.” It was simply untrue. The average member of the US military was no longer far above their average peer. The Bush administration, together with a pliant Pentagon (and the succeeding Obama administration), actually enabled the US military to undergo the biggest and fastest transformation in its history.And it was not just American soldiers who were short-changed by Rumsfeld’s vision — the occupied populations were sacrificed with rivers of their own blood. Many of the wars’ worst atrocities are linked directly to the loosening of enlistment regulations on criminals, racist extremists, and gang members, amongst others. Then there is the domestic US population, which has had to put up with military-trained gang members marauding around their cities; as well as Mexican civilians who have paid with their lives in the drug wars facilitated in part by the US military. The effects of this will be felt for decades to come. Finally, there’s the safety of the troops themselves. Loosening standards on intelligence and body weight, for example, compromised the military’s operational readiness and undoubtedly endangered the lives of American and allied troops. Thousands of young Americans have paid with their lives for this folly.US society changed profoundly after the September 11 attacks and during the subsequent wars. In many ways, the military is a reflection of the society from which it is drawn, and the changes in the composition of the US military and its regulations over this period reflected a country in political, cultural, and economic reverse gear. As America became increasingly bigoted and inward-looking, so radicalism in the US military increased. As young people became ever fatter, so too did the soldiers. As the criminal justice system locked more and more people up, so the military had to increase the numbers of felons it allowed in. ‘Today’s young men and women are more overweight . . . and are being charged for offenses that in earlier years wouldn’t have been considered a serious offense, and might not have resulted in charges in the first place’, an army spokesman complained in 2008. At the height of the War on Terror, only one in three men in the general population met the pre-9/11 physical, mental, educational, and other eligibility requirements needed to enlist in the armed forces. ‘The numbers of people who meet our enlistment standards is astonishingly low’, grumbled Under Secretary Dominguez at the end of his testimony lauding the military’s success. To enlist the rest, the US military had to change in profound and dangerous ways.This is an edited extract from Matt Kennard’s book Irregular Army: How the US Military Recruited Neo-Nazis, Gang Members, and Criminals, recently published by OR Books in a new and expanded edition.
content_html_zh:
2001 年 9 月 10 日，国防部长唐纳德·拉姆斯菲尔德站在五角大楼的伟人面前，发表了题为“从官僚主义到战场”的内容广泛的演讲。它的处方是激进的——是美国军事历史上最不祥的处方之一——但由于第二天的恐怖暴行，他的话仍然深深地埋在记忆洞里，而其后果则被埋在伊拉克和阿富汗的沙子之下。 “今天的话题是一个对美利坚合众国的安全构成威胁、严重威胁的对手”，拉姆斯菲尔德开始说道，然后透露威胁不是基地组织，而是“五角大楼官僚机构”。“不是人，而是过程”，他安慰地补充道。 “不是平民，而是系统。不是穿制服的男人和女人，而是我们经常强加给他们的思想和行动的统一。”本质上，拉姆斯菲尔德当天的讲话旨在为国防部服务的大规模私有化奠定基础并软化他的工人。这实现了共和党政客及其在华盛顿的企业盟友长期以来的梦想——他们现在将拥有一家甜品店，里面摆满了利润丰厚的政府人脉资源可供咀嚼。

随着 9/11 的悲惨事件以及随后发生的中东两线地面战争，拉姆斯菲尔德获得了在几乎没有反对的情况下实施他的计划的机会。反恐战争八年之后，结果就很明显了，当时国防部在伊拉克有 95,461 名私人承包商为其工作，而美国军事人员只有 95,900 名。当时，使用私人承包商的现象已经非常普遍，以至于巴拉克·奥巴马 (Barack Obama) 在 2008 年竞选时做出的结束这种做法的承诺成为了许多人的又一个违背的承诺。然而，尽管战争努力的私有化是许多记者（尤其是杰里米·斯卡希尔）广泛探讨的话题，但该计划的一个方面却很少受到报道——即拉姆斯菲尔德为国防部工资单上的士兵制定的计划。这个计划对于军队和居住在他们手下的被占领居民来说将是灾难性的。拉姆斯菲尔德的计划以商业式的效率节约为幌子，旨在削弱美国军队，让美国军队成为美国很快将使用的庞大私人军队的附属力量。

“在这个资金有限的时期，”拉姆斯菲尔德继续说道，“我们需要每一分钱、每一个好主意、每一项创新、每一项努力来帮助美国军队实现现代化和转型。”这只能通过改变五角大楼工作方式的基础来实现，这个过程后来被称为“转型”：“正如你们所知，我们最需要的许多技能在私营部门也有很高的需求。”为了竞争，我们需要为国防部带来已经改变了私营部门的人力资源实践。”甚至国防部本身也要像公司一样运作：“我们必须采用现代商业工具。更灵活的薪酬方案、现代化的征兵技术和更好的训练。”拉姆斯菲尔德想要的是一支规模缩小、精简的美国军队——这是对鲍威尔主义的逆转，鲍威尔主义以沙漠风暴将军科林·鲍威尔的名字命名，他相信大量的部队数量、“压倒性的力量”和明确的退出战略。

对于拉姆斯菲尔德来说，这是一个冒险的做法。甚至在 9/11 之前，现任国务卿鲍威尔就观察到，“我们的武装部队相当薄弱，我们能够维持的部署数量是有限的。”事实证明，这只是轻描淡写的说法。但拉姆斯菲尔德的新做法得到了他的老板乔治·W·布什总统的支持，令鲍威尔惊愕的是，布什总统也有同样的愿景：“建设明天的军队并不容易。”布什在 2001 年 5 月表示，改变我们军队的方向就像改变一艘巨轮的航向。

人们经常重复的陈词滥调是，9 月 11 日一切都发生了变化，而且确实对数百万美国人来说发生了变化。但拉姆斯菲尔德却并非如此：他的优先事项保持不变，而当他被拍到帮助五角大楼袭击受害者送上救护车时，他的人气飙升。他现在不仅拥有改变这艘强大舰船航向和设计精简型商业式战争机器的终极掩护，而且还拥有进行实验的完美实验室。没过多久，战鼓很快就响起了。对阿富汗的入侵始于拉姆斯菲尔德启发的特种部队的任务，即贿赂当地军阀，并通过空袭来消除对压倒性人力的需求。但所谓的新保守派还没有结束：他们把目光投向了伊拉克的最终战利品，果然，十八个月后，更具争议性的是，这个拥有世界第三大石油储量的国家遭到了袭击——这是拉姆斯菲尔德的副手保罗·沃尔福威茨（Paul Wolfowitz）在 1990 年代一直倡导的举措（再次是拉姆斯菲尔德式的小规模部队）。在接下来的十年里，美国的炸弹落在叙利亚、巴基斯坦和也门等地，中东变成了一场但丁式的大火，平民、叛乱分子和美国军人都被卷入大火。

拉姆斯菲尔德的“灵活”军事计划新实验的实验对象是爱国的美国人，他们相信自己参军是为了保卫自己的国家——在反恐战争最激烈的时期，其中有 160 万人曾在中东服役。这个数字相当于爱沙尼亚这样的国家或费城这样的城市的人口，一定会让拉姆斯菲尔德感到惊讶，因为他的整个战争计划都是以小得多的兵力和短暂的战争为基础的。事实上，9/11 事件发生后不久，拉姆斯菲尔德请汤米·弗兰克斯将军估计入侵伊拉克需要多少部队。上一次入侵伊拉克应急计划是1998年制定的，建议兵力超过38万。但是，显然是迫于拉姆斯菲尔德的压力，弗兰克斯将军提出了他的“生成启动”计划，最初的部队人数仅为 275,000 人。即使这对拉姆斯菲尔德来说也太过分了，他以典型的专横方式打倒了将军，并推动了一支更小的部队。

据《纽约时报》军事记者迈克尔·戈登报道，“他们想出了一种叫做跑动起步的变体，你可以从一个师左右开始，然后增援部队会在它后面流动。”因此，你从小规模开始，但你只是不断地发送更多你需要的东西。”这种方法产生了所谓的“战争中的战争”，因为它让拉姆斯菲尔德与受欢迎的陆军参谋长埃里克·辛塞基将军和国务卿鲍威尔对立起来，他们两人（正确地）都认为，在最初的战斗结束后，需要大量部队来确保安全。新关将军因拉姆斯菲尔德在这个问题上的顽固态度而感到难以忍受，于是公开提议组建 20 万军队。没关系。拉姆斯菲尔德不听。

这种策略被意识形态蒙蔽了双眼，直接违背了入侵的表面目标——解除萨达姆·侯赛因的武装。迈克尔·戈登和伯纳德·特雷纳将军在他们的《眼镜蛇 II》一书中写道，“美国没有足够的军队来保护据称在伊拉克发现的数百个疑似大规模杀伤性武器地点，或保护该国漫长而漏洞百出的边界”。 “如果伊拉克人拥有大规模杀伤性武器，并且恐怖组织在伊拉克盛行，正如布什政府如此大声宣称的那样，那么（数量有限的）美军很可能无法阻止大规模杀伤性武器被偷偷带出该国并落入政府宣战的黑暗势力手中。”但是，造成美军及其战争目标随之受到损害的不仅仅是意识形态；还有更多的问题。也存在相当大的无能。 2002 年 8 月美国中央司令部解密的战争计划文件表明，布什政府对接下来的占领准备得多么不足。他们制定的计划假设，到 2006 年 12 月，美军将几乎完全从伊拉克撤军，仅留下 5,000 名剩余部队。这很疯狂，但对拉姆斯菲尔德来说却是音乐。

经过一番争吵后，拉姆斯菲尔德和弗兰克斯达成妥协，最初的兵力数量为 13 万人，比 20 世纪 90 年代的设想要少得多，但也没有拉姆斯菲尔德希望的那么少。无论如何，事情并没有按计划进行，而且军队的数量也不足以控制这个拥有3000万人口的国家。第一任总督保罗·布雷姆纳 (Paul Bremner) 解除警察和军队的职务引发了无休无止的暴力事件后，局势陷入混乱，越来越多的军人与私人承包商一起被派出。到2005年，美国在伊拉克部署了15万名士兵，在阿富汗部署了19,500名士兵。但战争计划意味着军方没有为这种部署做好任何准备——而且它正在瓦解。 2005年，伊拉克战争刚开始两年，人们就公开谈论美军已经到了崩溃的边缘。在当年 3 月的参议院听证会上，理查德·A·科迪 (Richard A. Cody) 将军公开表达了这些担忧：“让我彻夜难眠的是，这支全志愿部队在 2007 年会是什么样子。”但他只知道其中一半。更糟糕的是，同年，军队未能达到自1979年以来最大的征兵目标，当时美国社会仍受所谓“越南综合症”的困扰，军队规模要大得多，招募的士兵数量是1979年以来的两倍。

大约在这个时候，一位名叫安德鲁·F·克雷皮内维奇（Andrew F. Krepinevich）的退役军官根据五角大楼的合同撰写了一份令人震惊的报告，其中严厉批评美军能够在不破坏军队或输掉战争的情况下维持在伊拉克的兵力水平。他的诊断很简单：美国武装部队“面临着针对发动叛乱的非正规部队的长期部署”，但在阿富汗和伊拉克提供稳定和安全所需的地面部队“显然超过了执行任务所需的地面部队”。为了弥补这一点，陆军正在以牺牲部队为代价，通过后门进行变革。克雷皮内维奇指出，军人的频繁且不及时的重新部署。 “士兵和旅的部署频率和部署时间都超出了陆军认为适当的水平，以吸引和留住维持所需部队规模和质量所需的士兵数量。”他提出了三个解决方案来克服部队严重短缺的问题：更频繁地重新部署现有部队；重新部署它们的时间更长；或部署美国海军陆战队地面部队。

前两个处方已经由军队采取，但这是一场危险的游戏。克雷皮内维奇问道：“一名士兵在遭受危险的情况下，有多少次仍愿意留在军队？” “即使对陆军领导人来说，也不清楚这种做法可以持续多久而不引起招募和保留问题。”他的结论并不乐观：他说，陆军正在“与时间赛跑”，“其执行长期计划的能力”因“招募和保留灾难性下降而破坏军队”的风险而受到损害。

他继续说，很难不对现役和预备役部队造成“如此严重的压力，以至于招募和保留问题变得如此严重，以至于威胁到部队的有效性”。那时，每个人——甚至包括拉姆斯菲尔德本人——都知道军队必须扩大规模。长期以来一直主张扩大军队规模的众议员艾克·斯泰尔顿（Ike Stelton）表示，“当今的需求给我们的军人带来了如此高的压力，我们努力减轻他们的负担变得比以往任何时候都更加重要”。但随着招募人数的减少和对战争的普遍愤怒，这怎么可能做到呢？

有一种行动方案可以立即缝合军队的裂痕，那就是：征兵。但争议太大了。 1973 年越南战争结束时，国会废除了非自愿征兵制度。在克雷皮内维奇撰写本文时，它确实进入了全国性的讨论，尽管布什政府出于赤裸裸的自身利益的原因仍然坚决反对。 2005 年 4 月，当拉姆斯菲尔德在参议院拨款国防小组委员会作证时，民主党参议员丹尼尔·井上提出了这个问题。 “陆军和海军陆战队多年来第一次没有达到他们的征兵目标。有些人已经在讨论草案了”，他以外交机智说道。恼怒的拉姆斯菲尔德在座位上拖着脚步向前走，把嘴靠近麦克风：“我认为唯一可以想象的谈论草案的人是那些在近乎完全无知的情况下发言的人，”他回答道。 “我们最不需要的就是草稿。我们只是不这样做。

民主党一直在推动，直到纽约国会议员查尔斯·兰格尔 (Charles Rangel) 于 2006 年 2 月重新提出了该法案草案，如果该法案获得通过，将恢复所有 42 岁以下人群的征兵制。兰格尔说：“正如总统在国情咨文中宣布的那样，在伊朗、朝鲜和叙利亚，军事选项每天都摆在桌面上，无论我们喜欢与否，恢复军事征兵都是一个必须考虑的选项。” “如果军队已经很难招募到所需的新兵，那么当战争从伊拉克蔓延到其他国家时，我们能做些什么来填补空缺呢？”除了草案之外，我们可能别无选择。”该草案被国会否决，这让布什总统非常高兴。他事后表示，“我对众议院坚决拒绝​​‘恢复草案’法案表示赞赏”。 “如果这项法案提交给我，我会否决它。美国的全志愿军队是世界上最好的，恢复征兵制将是一项糟糕的政策。我们增加了工资和福利，以确保我们的部队拥有战斗和赢得反恐战争所需的资源。我希望每个美国人都明白，只要我是总统，就不会有征兵。

恢复征兵制并不会让美国在其盟友中显得异常：许多国家仍然实行强制征兵计划，包括以色列和（直到 2011 年）德国。然而，在一场如此不受欢迎、总统承受如此大压力的战争中，政府意识到征召全国年轻人入伍很可能成为压垮骆驼的最后一根稻草。征兵被拒绝后，军方又采用了越南战争期间早期扩充兵力的方法：不仅在社会最贫困的人群中招募士兵，而且还改变了征兵规定。 1965年，随着南越军队规模的扩大，军队开始放弃征兵标准，首次录取了数十万智商最低的男子。在美国在越南升级行动之前，这些人通常会被拒绝，但随着一场针对“新标准”的战争，这些人突然被宣布适合参战。拒绝率直线下降”，一位历史学家写道。 1966年，时任国防部长罗伯特·麦克纳马拉制定了一项名为“10万计划”的计划，旨在通过接纳以前不符合要求标准的平民，在两年内将部队数量增加到这个数字。

由于没有征兵，而且招募目标一直未能实现，布什政府和五角大楼制定了类似的计划。通过将大量的作战任务分包给黑水公司和戴恩公司等私人军事机构，政府已经部分解决了炮灰需求与优质军队供应之间的缺口。一个薄弱的国家联盟，后来包括驻阿富汗的北约部队和驻伊拉克的联合国部队，帮助改善了长期的部队赤字。但这还不够。 2004年，布什采取了另一个不同寻常的步骤来填补这个漏洞：他召集了37,000名国民警卫队成员前往伊拉克沙漠作战。自朝鲜战争以来，还没有进行过这样的动员：国民警卫队曾在海湾战争和科索沃服役，但人数远没有如此之多。到2005年6月，他们占伊拉克军队总数的45%。然后是金钱诱因，这对军人来说非常有利可图，但对五角大楼来说却造成了损害。兰德公司进行了一项深入研究，发现国防部用于入伍和重新入伍奖金的预算在 2000 年至 2008 年间猛增，增加了一倍多，达到 14 亿美元。同期，每名士兵的平均入伍奖金从 5,600 美元增加到约 18,000 美元。从2006年到2009年，短短三年时间里，军队就花费了10亿美元，招募了64,526名现役和预备役士兵。陆军预备役新兵的奖金同期增加了一倍多，达到 19,500 美元。但研究还发现，在军队中，征兵人员本身往往是比入伍奖金或加薪更具成本效益的扩大军队队伍的方式。布什政府也走了这条路。为了实现2007年的目标，陆军增加了8,000名新兵，并新增了2,000名助理人员。

与此同时——也许最重要的是——那些本应被赶出去的人被允许留下来。 2005年6月，《华尔街日报》向高级指挥官提交了一份内部备忘录，其中称日益增长的辍学率——军事术语中称为“自然减员”——是“一个令人高度关注的问题”。上面写道：“我们需要你们共同努力扭转负面趋势”。 “通过减少 1% 的减员，我们可以节省多达 3,000 名初期士兵。我们的部队里又多了 3,000 名士兵。”这是一个明确的呼吁，要求放弃标准：传达的信息是，酗酒、吸毒、失去健康或精神失常的士兵不应该被开除。孵化时间已经被压平了。 《华尔街日报》援引一名营长的话说：“是控制体重的人。” 。 。学校缺席、吸毒者等等，他们占用了我的时间，导致我的头发过早变白。 。 。他们常常同时遇到不止一个这些问题。

这种情绪并非凭空存在。事实上，拉姆斯菲尔德和五角大楼允许废除美国武装部队自越战以来 25 年来建立的整个征兵和留用监管结构。军队本来就单薄，需要增肥，而这是唯一的办法。最终，它对谁有资格在美国武装部队服役进行了彻底的重新评估，这是美国现代历史上闻所未闻的一次全面改造。 1990年第一次海湾战争相对平静的日子里，美军阻止了重罪犯的入伍。它排斥智商低或没有高中文凭的男性和女性。它要么阻止新纳粹分子和帮派成员入伍，要么将其驱逐。它将治疗或释放酗酒者、吸毒者和精神病患者。它将放弃外国公民为战争提供的服务。不再。尽管布什政府几乎普遍采取了保守政策，但它保留了美国军队的自由主义配给，废除了所有以前神圣不可侵犯的关于招募世界上最强大的战斗部队的规定。在反恐战争的支持下，美国武装部队成为美国“不同”、怪异和美妙（和危险）的圣地。

然而，在这一切过程中，军方对公众始终保持着一种平静、一切井然有序的微笑。直到 2007 年 8 月，当降临到该机构的危机广为人知时，负责人事和战备事务的国防部首席副部长迈克尔·多明格斯在国会听证会上作证时，一开始就对美国军方进行了批评：“首先让我承认这是一项历史性成就，几年前，许多人，包括我们自己的专家，都认为这是不可能的”，他说。 “我们把一支全志愿军带入了战争。我们在经济强劲、失业率为 4.5% 的情况下做到了这一点。我们经常要求该部队及其家人在短时间内采取更多行动。通过这一切，我们为国家军队配备的人员远远高于同龄人的平均水平。”这根本就是不真实的。美国军人的平均水平不再远高于其他军人的平均水平。布什政府与顺从的五角大楼（以及继任的奥巴马政府）实际上使美国军队经历了其历史上最大、最快的变革。

被拉姆斯菲尔德的愿景所欺骗的不仅仅是美国士兵——被占领的人民也被自己的血流成河牺牲了。战争中许多最严重的暴行都与放松对罪犯、种族主义极端分子和帮派成员等入伍规定直接相关。然后是美国国内民众，他们不得不忍受受过军事训练的帮派成员在他们的城市周围进行抢劫。以及在美国军方部分推动的毒品战争中付出生命代价的墨西哥平民。其影响将持续数十年。最后，还有部队本身的安全。例如，放松情报和体重标准会损害军队的作战准备状态，并且无疑会危及美国及其盟军的生命。成千上万的美国年轻人为这种愚蠢行为付出了生命的代价。

9 月 11 日袭击事件以及随后的战争期间，美国社会发生了深刻的变化。从很多方面来说，军队是其所在社会的反映，这一时期美国军队的构成及其法规的变化反映了一个国家在政治、文化和经济方面的倒退。随着美国变得越来越偏执和内向，美军中的激进主义也随之增加。随着年轻人越来越胖，士兵们也越来越胖。随着刑事司法系统关押了越来越多的人，军方不得不增加允许入境的重罪犯数量。“今天的年轻男女更加超重……”。 。 。一名陆军发言人在 2008 年抱怨道：“他们所犯的罪行在早些年不会被视为严重罪行，也可能不会导致指控。”在反恐战争最激烈的时期，普通民众中只有三分之一的男性符合 9/11 事件之前入伍所需的身体、心理、教育和其他资格要求。 “符合我们入伍标准的人数少得惊人”，副部长多明计斯在赞扬军队成功的证词结束时抱怨道。为了招募其他人，美国军队必须以深刻而危险的方式进行变革。

这是马特·肯纳德 (Matt Kennard) 的书《非正规军队：美国军队如何招募新纳粹分子、帮派成员和罪犯》的编辑摘录，该书最近由 OR Books 出版了新的扩展版。
